7.reason: Error: scanf("%s", s) should be used instead of scanf("%s", &s) because s is already an array (pointer to the first element). Using &s passes the address of the array, which is technically the same address but of a different type (pointer to array), leading to undefined behavior or compiler warnings.

reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-1: 1.0 , reason:  Error: scanf("%s", s) should be used instead of scanf("%s", &s) because s is already an array (pointer to the first element). Using &s passes the address of the array, which is technically the same address but of a different type (pointer to array), leading to undefined behavior or compiler warnings.
reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-2: 1.0 , reason:  Error: scanf("%s", s) should be used instead of scanf("%s", &s) because s is already a pointer to the first element of the array.
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-1: 0.25 , reason:  [Error: scanf expects a char*, but &s is of type char(*)[10000]; use s instead]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-2: 0.5 , reason:  [Error: scanf expects a char* argument, but &s is of type char(*)[10000]; should be scanf("%s", s)]
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-1: 0.5 , reason:  Incorrect argument type for %s format specifier; should be 's' instead of '&s', which causes a type mismatch compilation error/warning.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.5 , reason:  [Compilation Error: 's' is an array name that decays to a pointer. '&s' passes a pointer to an array (char (*)[10000]) instead of a char pointer, causing a type mismatch with '%s'. Should be 's'.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 1.0 , reason:  [Using &s is incorrect; s is already a pointer to char array, should be scanf("%s", s);]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.5 , reason:  [Using &s is incorrect; s is already a pointer to char array, should be scanf("%s", s);]
